pages.full_text:
The valley walk down was very, very beautiful with running water all the way, sheep, some cows, & we paddled in the stream.

    There is an old, disused road which runs along one end of the campsite. It leads up to a tumble-down house with an inglenook & a pump in the garden.

    Simon cooked lamb chops on the barbque in the evening, after John & Charles had gone to bed. It was a very hot day.

    On the Sunday we walked along to road to St. Michael's church. It had a ~~patchwork~~ cross stitch sampler on the wall in memory of a parishoner. The rector was ill so there was no servic[e]
pages.notes: The word 'patchwork' appears struck through (strikethrough correction) before 'cross stitch sampler'. Text cuts off at bottom of page mid-word 'servic[e]'. 'barbque' is as written — likely abbreviation of 'barbecue'. 'parishoner' is as written (possibly misspelling of 'parishioner').

pages.full_text:
Thomas dashed back to the campsite to tell Grandma & Grandpa, they had gone to the farm, as Thomas discovered when he asked passerbys, "have you seen 2 people over 70?"

    We drove of to the Boiling Well along a precarious singe track road up the side of a hill "under 2 tons." Mist was sweeping across the moorland and it became very cold. So we moved on to lower ground for lunch. On the way [S]imon scraped the side of the van while passing a car.

    Margaret had made us a delicious lunch with salads & summer pudding.
pages.notes: 'drove of' is as written (likely 'drove off'). 'singe track road' is as written (likely 'single track road'). 'passerbys' is as written. The Boiling Well is a notable place name — possibly a local spring or landmark on moorland. The left edge of the right page has partial words visible in the gutter overlap; these belong to the left page and are not transcribed here.
